text: Single incision robot-assisted donor nephrectomy. This is the case of a twenty-one year old healthy female who was evaluated for kidney donation to her father who suffered from end-state renal disease. Impression of the CTU angiogram was normal with regard to the renal anatomy.

A single core device is placed in a 5 cm subumbilical incision, followed by insertion of two 12 mm and two 8 mm trocars. The robotic cart is positioned from the patient's left, with the assistant on the right. The surgeon uses 8 mm instruments, including an atraumatic bipolar grasper and hook electrocautery, crossing instruments inside the abdomen for maneuverability. The assistant uses an atraumatic grasper to expose the left kidney by providing counter traction on the left descending colon. The left colon is mobilized, and the splenic flexure is taken down using hook electrocautery.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter in the pelvis and loops a six-centimeter Penrose drain around it, securing it with a clip. Using hook electrocautery, the ureter is circumferentially dissected along the gonadal vein. Girota's fascia is incised superiorly, revealing the kidney surface. A cephalodirectional plane is developed between the kidney and the adrenal gland, and the kidney is completely separated from its attachments to the spleen.
The surgeon dissects the ureter superiorly, preserving a generous amount of surrounding fat. Following the gonadal vein, the left renal vein is identified.

The surgeon dissects and divides the gonadal vein close to the renal vein using lock and clips. Similarly, the adrenal vein is dissected and divided between clips. Attention is then turned to the hilum of the kidney, where the renal vein is circumferentially dissected using hook cautery. Three posterior lumbar veins are dissected and clipped.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to circumferentially isolate and dissect the renal artery back to its origin at the aorta, employing wristed movements for precise manipulation.

After completing the dissection of the hilum of the left kidney, the robotic instruments are removed, and the procedure continues laparoscopically using the same trocars and a ten millimeter thirty degree laparoscope. The kidney is flipped medially and mobilized posteriorly with the NCL device, showing it free from all attachments except the vascular pedicles. The ureter is divided distally with two locking clips. The left renal artery is exposed, and an endo-TA stapler is placed proximally on the renal artery.

A ten millimeter clip is placed on the staple line, followed by scissors dividing the renal artery. The kidney is flipped back to its original position, exposing the left renal vein. An endo-GIA vascular cartridge is used to divide the renal vein. The gel point single port device is removed, and the surgeon's hand is introduced into the abdomen to retrieve the kidney. The initial incision is enlarged from five to seven centimeters to accommodate the surgeon's hand. Hemostasis is achieved, and the dissection areas show no bleeding. The abdomen is closed.

The surgical time was a hundred and fifty minutes, warm ischemia time three minutes, estimated blood loss seventy-five milliliters. The patient was discharged from the hospital seventy-two hours after surgery without complication.
